This is not currently supported in the pythonic API (until I find time to do it, which might be during the next 24 hours or next year - in the second case, patches welcome).

But you can do it quite easily with Gio's API:
```
# bus is a pydbus's SystemBus/SessionBus.
bus.con.signal_subscribe("sender_name", "iface_name", "signal_name", "object_path", None, 0, lambda *args: print(args))

from gi.repository import GObject
GObject.MainLoop().run()
```

Full documentation of signal_subscribe method is available at https://developer.gnome.org/gio/stable/GDBusConnection.html#g-dbus-connection-signal-subscribe

Hey!

With `GNU ar` the `-u` modifier is ignored as it isn't compatible with the default deterministic mode it uses.  Deterministic mode zeros all the timestamps so therefore `ar` can't determine file ages for the replacement behaviour of `-u`.  This means that this error message is produced: 

    ar: `u' modifier ignored since `D' is the default (see `U')

You can add the `-U` modifier to run `ar` in non-deterministic mode and maintain timestamps (and thus the replacement behaviour of `-u`) or remove the `-u` flag and keep deterministic archiving.

Cheers
추가하신 후처리 함수에서 noun+pos_feature 인 경우 noun만 남기는 방식을 취하고 있는데, pos_feature만 추출된 경우는 후처리 함수를 통해 제거되지 않습니다. 그 결과, '으로', '에서'와 같이 pos_feature에 해당하는 단어가 명사로 추출되는 것으로 확인됩니다. 후처리 함수를 작성하실 때 참고하시면 좋을 것 같습니다.

Not sure if this is feasible. After all, the browser would have to redraw every box every so and so many milliseconds. If you're looking at ordinary `width="100%"` elements, no browser I know of redraws upon window size change. The user has to reload to page to adapt widths. Same with tuna right now. That said, we could give all `rect`s relative widths, in case a browser supports redrawing upon window resize one day.

Hi @B-Stefan,

Thanks a lot for contributing!

Bundles provide a mechanism to package PROV records and then to *talk* about the package's provenance. Only then we need to have a bundle entity. See [an example](https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-dm/#anexample-provenance-of-provenance).
Therefore, I don't think we need to introduce a bundle entity record for every bundle as a matter of fact.

In addition, one design goal for this library (that I have in mind) is *consistency*, i.e. you will get back what you give the library, nothing more or less. By introducing new assertions, the library essentially changes the underlying information it processes, which I don't think it should do.

(Even when the information is invalid, I've been trying to maintain 1-1 mapping between inputs and outputs or raise an exception when it's not possible)

On another note, unification is not a complete feature yet as it only relies on identifiers and ignores [the constraints specified by the PROV model](https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-constraints/). Any suggestion on this front is very welcome.

SSL and TLS are usually used interchangeably, as TLS is newer replacement for SSL, basically a new version. TLS is frequently referred to as SSL (including in the Python standard library), but all versions of SSL now have known vulnerabilities, so the future will hopefully by TLS only - that's why I went with use_tls for the argument name.

Servers supporting SSL/TLS can do so via an encrypted connection (SSL/TLS handshake on connect) or a plaintext connection followed by STARTTLS. The resulting encryption is the same.

Hi @rosivda, thanks for the contribution.
Unfortunately I think there is a problem with this function. What happens if there are the exact byte sequences that `re.finditer` is looking for but not as the records you suppose they are? For example, if coincidentally some polygon coordinate results exactly in those bytes? I believe you have to consider the inner structure of the GDSII format and read record by record. In fact I believe the `tutorial.gds` file that accompanies gdspy already falls in this category. If I print the lists `start` and `end` I find they have different sizes!

How about something like this:
```python
def gdsii_hash(fname, engine=None):
    with open(fname, 'rb') as fin:
        data = fin.read()
    contents = []
    pos = 0
    while pos < len(data):
        size, rec = struct.unpack('>HH', data[pos:pos+4])
        if rec == 0x0502:
            start = pos + 28
        elif rec == 0x0700:
            contents.append(data[start:pos])
        pos += size
    h = hashlib.sha1() if engine is None else engine
    for x in sorted(contents):
        h.update(x)
    return h.hexdigest()
```
It seems to find only the correct records, but it does run about 2× slower as far as I can tell.

Is `@asyncio.coroutine` equivalent to using the `async / await` keywords? There are a couple of issues to work around:

1. Those keywords are a syntax error in older versions, so you can't import or define code that uses them at all into Py<=3.4-compatible modules.
2. If you need to add `async / await` or `@types.coroutine / yield from` to every method, you'll need a second copy of every method of every implementation (i.e. "doubles the surface area") that's the same interface, but async.
3. Even if you could find a way to avoid doing that, implicit behavior changes are bad: there _should_ be an `AsyncStatsClient` and `AsyncTCPStatsClient` and...

You should have to deliberately opt into the async interface—nothing should suddenly change behavior unexpectedly. That means async-enabled versions of every existing interface—but they have to be imported from somewhere distinct because they can't reliably be imported into Py2-compatible modules. And is there a strong reason to have `from statsd.async import AsyncStatsClient` instead of `from statsd_async import AsyncStatsClient`?

take this flask skeleton for example:
```python
from flask import Flask, request

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/webhook_path', methods=['GET', 'POST'])
def pass_update():
    update_queue.put(request.data)  # dump data to queue

here instead of returning OK we will return the json : 
```python
from flask import Flask, request

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/webhook_path', methods=['GET', 'POST'])
def pass_update():
    query_id, from_id, query = glance(request.data['inline_query'], flavor='inline')
    return bot.answerInlineQuery(query_id, [InlineQueryResultArticle(
                id='1',
                text=query ,
                title='Current Unix timestamp',
    )], return_response=True)
```

of course in a real application we have to check if it is an inline query before doing this and make sure its thread safe and all that, but for the sake of the example i just assumed flask is running a single thread and bot only receives inline queries.

it is not the perfect solution because of many reasons, not being able to use queues and not getting the result and all, but we are not forcing it, just as an extra way of doing it and to support all telegram bot api features.

I think it's worth re-evaluating this. The recent intermediate certificate change has caused issues for a number of users who were using the defaults suggested by this project (such as the renewal script).

I'm all in favour of keeping this script as small as possible; there are plenty of clients who cater to users with other requirements, after all. But this is an essential feature: Certificates are practically useless without a certificate chain. Automation, which is basically the main goal of ACME, is basically impossible to deploy with `acme-tiny` because ACME servers make no guarantees whatsoever about issuer certificates staying the same for any period of time.

My proposal wouldn't change the current default behavior of `acme-tiny` and could likely be implemented in <= 10 LOC. I don't think it introduces any unnecessary complexity - it merely implements an integral part of the ACME specification.

I would not implement something like that as part of the 'range' feature as you indicate because you would have to enter that complicated 'range' URL every time you wanted to update it, too. URL ranges are not saved(although there are `first_chapter`/`last_chapter` settings you can put in `personal.ini` that are equivalent). A `personal.ini` setting for a list of chapter URLs to ignore is more workable, but would require refactoring how the chapter list is created and `numChapters` set across all adapters. I would lean towards chapter URLs vs numbers because some authors/sites insert chapters in the 'middle' relative to the entire list--especially xenforo threadmarks where all Sidestory chapters come after Threadmark chapters, etc. Recording chapter numbers to ignore isn't necessarily easy either--the example story you gave doesn't show chapter numbers on the source site. The problem is the abstract contains `..\{-...` , which comes from https://github.com/jayvdb/bibutils-archive/blame/master/lib/latex.c#L27 .

`bibparse.py` recognises that as increasing the depth of braces, and fails to find the end.

The real problem here is that it doesnt check whether the end of file was reached, which #59 addresses.

Then we can also use a one character look behind buffer to not increase the depth when encountering the escaped `\{` .

@jayspang that method isn't really well named. The url that's being returned is not the url for the snapshot, but the url for the video stream for the camera you passed to it.

In order to call the api TakeSnapshot() uses under the covers you have start the camera video feed. This function mimics what happens when you start recording video in the UI and then click the camera icon to take a snapshot. So, really... It's more like "TriggerStreamSnapshot()". :)

If you just want to take a snapshot, you should use TriggerFullFrameSnapshot(), which doesn't require the camera video feed to be active.

Try this:
```
from Arlo import Arlo

USERNAME = 'arlo_user@mail.com'
PASSWORD = 'supersecret'

try:
    arlo = Arlo(USERNAME, PASSWORD)
    camera = arlo.GetDevices('camera')[0]
    basestation = arlo.GetDevices('basestation')[0]
    url = arlo.TriggerFullFrameSnapshot(basestation, camera)
    arlo.DownloadSnapshot(url, 'snapshot.jpg')
except Exception as e:

Hi Mark,

The information that you can get is generally made available via the member variables  in the YANGDynClass metaclass:

```
  """
    Wrap an type - specified in the base_type arugment - with
    a set of custom attributes that YANG specifies (or are required
    for serialisation of that object). Particularly:

      - base_type:  the original type - for example, string, int.
      - default:    the YANG specified default value of the type.
      - yang_name:  the YANG name of the type (as opposed to a 'safe'
                    Python version).
      - parent:     the class which this type is a member of in the
                    YANG-specified tree.
      - choice:     The choice branch that this type is a member of.
      - is_{container,leaf}: whether this element is a container or
                             a leaf.
      - path_helper: pyangbind helper class to allow XPATH lookups.
      - supplied_register_path: an override for the path that this
                                object should register to. This is
                                used when an element is a member of
                                a list to add the key attributes to
                                the path.
      - extensions:  The list of extensions that should be stored
                     with the type.
  """
```

This in general does not carry all of the YANG information (`base_type` will store the class that is being wrapped, so this potentially has some of the information). I'm not quite clear of the use case that one would have for having all that information available - since instantiating the type should validate this information internally.

For example, let's say you did want to get the restrictions that are associated with something with a 'pattern' or 'range' argument with it, then you can get some of this info based on the fact that the `RestrictedClass` dynamically generated type stores this information as class variables:

```
>>> from bindings import int_
>>> x = int_()
>>> x.int_container.eightrestricted
0
>>> print x.int_container.eightrestricted._restriction_dict
{'range': [u'-42..10']}
```

In this case `base_type` for YANGDynClass is not interesting at all:

```
>>> print x.int_container.eightrestricted._base_type
<class 'lib.yangtypes.RestrictedClass'>
```

If you wanted to implement this, then you could store something like the member of the class_map dict member which does have all this information along with the base class. I wouldn't really want this to be the default behaviour though unless there's a strong use case for it.

Cheers,
r.

I have the same problem (with OAuth1) and using the contrib client almost works.
The client relies on the `OAuth1Session` from `requests-oauthlib` which does a POST call in` _fetch_token` without the ability to pass any parameter such as the certificate to use for verification. (See [here](https://github.com/requests/requests-oauthlib/blob/master/requests_oauthlib/oauth1_session.py#L340)).
And while I did not  test it, it appears that the same problem is present with `OAuth2Session` (See [here](https://github.com/requests/requests-oauthlib/blob/master/requests_oauthlib/oauth2_session.py#L176))

Thankfully, lukasa over at `#python-requests` suggested a nice work around to specify the certificate. Requests uses `certifi` which we can mock to return the path to the certificate you need. My code looks like this:
```python
import sys

class FakeCertifi(object):
    def where(self):
        return '<path/to/your/cert.crt>'

sys.modules['certifi'] = FakeCertifi()
```
I will open an issue directly to `requests-oauthlib` and hopefully they will provide an option for the certificate's path. And then it would be great if Flask-OAuthlib could expose the option as well, at least in the contrib client.

There is one problem if we pass in the swarm as an optional parameter. If you use the `GeneralOptimizer` you'd have to do something like this:
```python
import pyswarms as ps
# Other code
...
swarm = ps.backend.swarms.Swarm(params)
my_topology = Ring(static=True, swarm)
ps.single.GeneralOptimizer(func, swarm, topology=my_toplogy)
```
Which, in my opinion, adds an extra layer of complexity for the user. How about this, we have a `neighbor_idx`(=None by initialization) and a `static` attribute in the topology class. In the `compute_gbest()` method we check if the static parameter is `True` and if `neighbor_idx` is set to decide if we compute the neighbors again:

```python
# in compute_gbest()
if self.static and neighbor_idx is None:
   # Set neighbor_idx
elif not self.static:
   # Set neighbor_idx
```

So in the former, the `neighbor_idx` is only set in the first method call and in the latter, it's set in every method call. We have the swarm and the topology separated and a very easy solution like that.

IMO if anyone considers to improve the library I would go with the library `cryptography`. It looks like that's what the general Python library is steering towards.

https://github.com/pyca/cryptography

IMO a lot of the functionality of `PyCrypto` (if not all) could be used replaced with `cryptography`.

```
>>> from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization
>>> from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
>>> k = serialization.load_ssh_public_key(PUB_KEY, default_backend())
>>> k
<cryptography.hazmat.backends.openssl.rsa._RSAPublicKey object at 0x7fdf421eed30>
>>> k.key_size
2048
>>> dir(k)
['__class__', '__delattr__', '__dict__', '__dir__', '__doc__', '__eq__', '__format__', '__ge__', '__getattribute__', '__gt__', '__hash__', '__init__', '__le__', '__lt__', '__module__', '__ne__', '__new__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__setattr__', '__sizeof__', '__str__', '__subclasshook__', '__weakref__', '_backend', '_evp_pkey', '_key_size', '_rsa_cdata', 'encrypt', 'key_size', 'public_bytes', 'public_numbers', 'verifier', 'verify']
>>> k = serialization.load_ssh_public_key(b'foobar', default_backend())
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/dave/cloudscale/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/cryptography/hazmat/primitives/serialization.py", line 40, in load_ssh_public_key
    'Key is not in the proper format or contains extra data.')
ValueError: Key is not in the proper format or contains extra data.
```

`PUB_KEY` in this case is the content of a simple `id_rsa.pub` (or dsa/ecdsa) file. I have only tried it with `.rsa` files, but i don't see why that would not be working with other files as well.

It probably doesn't validate `RFC4716` keys (keys starting with `---- BEGIN SSH2 PUBLIC KEY ----`) this way, but I'm not sure that this is what you want in this library anyway (I would suggest an alternate API endoint than the default one). 

IMO the `key_size` is really all you need in your library and therefore it should be quite easy to replace.

> API Wishlist?
> 
> just curious what your wishlist for the client/WS API is - perhaps I can add them to the witness_node or cli_wallet.

There is an API wishlist: https://github.com/cryptonomex/graphene/issues/382
Essentially, I would like to provide a simple library that offers the same calls as poloniex .. so that people can simply plug&play their poloniex-capable bots into bitshares



> Library Architecture?
> 
> Also, related - what do you envision as the overall architecture of the library, in the end? Would you want to create an even higher level abstraction of the current API?

I usually keep things close together: `*base` is the core functionalities of bitshares like address, pubkey, wif key stuff and later transaction signing. `*api` is obviously a folder for  api related stuff and `*extra` would be for libraries that abstract things away from the end user .. like proposed transfers, or maybe 3rd party 'plugins' .. But in the end, this is a growing project and so can the requirements and the organization.




> Documentation?
> 
> It could be good to document how the library interacts with everything. This is my understanding so far:
> 1) the api class can connect to cli_wallet and implements all RPC commands

yes

> 2) the ws api class is a subclass of api, connects to witness_node via authenticated ws and also via unauthenticated RPC commands

yes, though there is an issue I identified earlier last week.
The idea was to have an API for the cli_wallet (RPC) and one for the witness_node (websocket). However the witness node can also answer RPC requests (though has a different set of available calls). That's why the RPC class is a base class and the websocket class a child class.
However, if I connect to a websocket server using a URL like bitshares.openledger.info/ws (which is proxied to the 'real' API), the RPC part of the websocket connection will not work properly. Haven't figured out a way to fix this yet, not sure it can even be fixed "nicely". Do you have a recommendation how to separated RPC and Websockets properly?



> 3) the wsprotocol implements asynchronous message handling.



> awesome features:
> 
> 1) the wsprotocol has a class attribute of objectMap, which is a dictionary of every object you have looked in to - and it is updated every block as needed! Word of caution: orders (1.7.*) are deleted from the blockchain when they are filled, but they are not deleted from the objectMap as no notification is ever sent by witness_node when they are deleted! (cryptonomex/graphene#488) Also, when you use getObject or getObjectcb, the library checks objectMap first - saving extra calls!

Thanks for highlighting the issue with 1.7.*. Haven't looked into it yet.
I think we can easily force the library to poll those 1.7.* objects if they are used. The whole getObject idea is not yet implemented to my satisfaction yet either. It's on the list of things to improve




> Some things I learned:
> 
> 1) objectMap is shared among all instances of the wsprotocol class, so even though the ws api only stores the class (not the instance), it will have access to objectMap. Instances are generated by autobahn's websocket factory.

Yes. That was the intension. Though I have never really looked into the side effects of having several instances running

> 2) the self.api_ids is not shared among all instances of the wsprotocol class. This causes problems if you try to connect to a restricted API in anything except a subclass of your wsprotocol

Good point. If you add it to 
https://github.com/xeroc/python-graphenelib/blob/master/grapheneapi/graphenewsprotocol.py#L15-L21
It will be shared as well.

> 3) That said, you can "trick" python into giving you the actual instance of the protocol if you set a callback to function in another class. This causes both your new class and the protocol to be passed as the first and second arguments to the method in the new class, thus giving you access to self.api_ids

You can share variables across instances by using 'static' variables
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/68645/static-class-variables-in-python

You simply have to define the variable right after `class ClassName():`




> A complex use case
> 
> I need something with both cli_wallet and witness_node interface, and I want to get the actual protocol passed back to me. I can do something like this:
> 1) Create a new class
> That means we don't have to make our own subclass of GrapheneWebsocketProtocol, instead make GrapheneWebsocketProtocol cannonical for all users, and have them just make classes that have the APIs as attributes of the class

This would be another abstraction layer and I think I can write a new `GrapheneWebsocketProtocol` that does exactly this with a template for the `botClass` and it's sub routines.

Jinja2 does not provide line numbers for all exceptions, but some types of `TemplateError`s are tagged with a line number attribute.  The script in this pull request demonstrates what happens if you add an extraneous `endif` to a Word file.

If you forget an `endif`, Jinja2 does not report a line number because it just scans until the end of the file.  Also if your Python expression triggers an exception, the line number is apparently not reported.

@kevinthesun 
> If you don't know exactly what a model does and what kind of inputs it accepts, it might not be a good idea to directly use it in your app.

I understand your concerns, but the sole purpose of my app is to provide a simple way to expose models as web service.
If I train model with dataset of int32 and 300x300 shape, I think that is obvious that I intend to use it with the same shape and datatype. The point is, why mxnet doesn't provide information about datatypes and shapes, which are already inferred and known in the training process, when I export model?

For instance, in TensorFlow:
1. I create Signature for a model with Tensor information (as described in https://www.tensorflow.org/serving/serving_basic). 
2. I specify which tensors are inputs and which ones are outputs. Shapes and types, defined statically or inferred, are written to the signature. 
3. Signature information is exported with model itself.

When I import a model, I know about model inputs/outputs, with their types and shapes at a runtime. This helps me to validate data and pass it to the model. This also gives me an opportunity to create a documentation or an interface, so external users are able to use this model.

In conclusion, mxnet doesn't have a Signature mechanism and it infers shape and data at the training. So, why doesn't it save this information along with model?

Hi @jgao54 

Im not sure if deleting the signal handler from the —raw task is the right thing to do. The signal handler is there to trigger the on_kill method of the operator. If absent that would not be run anymore. bashOperator and the SparkSubmitOperator both make use of this.

On your comment of the “root” process. I don’t think you are correct and your implementation worked by coincidence. If you run “bash -c sleep 1000”, you will find ‘sleep’ in your process list but you won’t find ‘bash’ running anymore. However the parent pid of sleep still refers to the pid of bash. So your implementation worked in case of a TaskRunner that used something like bash to execute the command. If you would you “sleep” directly (so without bash) you would get ourselves as the parent of the “sleep” process and thus kill yourself.

Using setsid makes sure we Airflow sees all processes to the new session/group regardless. Except when they run setsid themselves, which happens for example in the BashOperator. The bashOperator therefore becomes responsible for cleaning up after itself.

In that case, the old loader kicks-in and `eval()` converts it into an int, and then tait chocks on it.

Simple test code:
```python
class MyApp(Application):
    a = Unicode().tag(config=True)

MyApp().initialize(['--MyApp.a=1234'])

[MyApp] CRITICAL | Bad config encountered during initialization:
[MyApp] CRITICAL | The 'a' trait of a MyApp instance must be a unicode string, but a value of 1234 <class 'int'> was specified.
```

The other benefit here is that it provides a public API for blocking cross-validation.  For example, in serializable-traitlets, I have a notion of "example values" that are validated in a way similarly to default values, which requires holding the cross validation lock to ensure that we don't fire cross-validators when checking that an example is valid.  That's essentially the same thing that's happening in https://github.com/ipython/traitlets/pull/166/files#diff-8dc419d0669d108df4ac215bf816cc43R491, but it's that's a tenuous implementation detail to rely on in an external project.

ec2.meta.events.register('before-call', inject_headers)

ec2.describe_instances()

from pyVim.connect import SmartConnect, Disconnect
import ssl
import atexit
from pyVmomi import vim, vmodl, VmomiSupport
import time


def connect():
    context = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv23)
    context.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE

    si = SmartConnect(host='xx.xx.xx.xx', user='administrator@vsphere.local', pwd='password', port=443, sslContext=context)
    atexit.register(Disconnect, si)
    content = si.RetrieveContent()
    return content

def get_obj(content, vimtype, name):
    """
    Return an object by name, if name is None the
    first found object is returned
    """
    obj = None
    if name in ['', None]:
        obj = []
    container = content.viewManager.CreateContainerView(content.rootFolder, vimtype, True)
    for c in container.view:
        if name:
            if c.name == name:
                obj = c
                break
        else:
            obj.append(c)

    container.Destroy()
    return obj

content = connect()

host = get_obj(content, [vim.HostSystem], None)[0]
option_manager = host.configManager.advancedOption
option = vim.option.OptionValue()
option.key = 'UserVars.ESXiShellInteractiveTimeOut'
option.value = VmomiSupport.vmodlTypes['long'](12)
option_manager.UpdateOptions(changedValue=[option])

I think one of the things that still isn't clear to a lot of folks is that this proposal is mainly a competitor to the current approach of using utilities like `pip freeze` and `pip-tools` ( https://github.com/nvie/pip-tools#readme ) as a way of generating requirements.txt files. The latter is my own current preferred solution, but you still end up with a lot of custom scaffolding around it (especially if you have multiple requirements.txt files covering different subprojects).

Many (most?) of the projects using these tools don't even have a `setup.py` anywhere, as they're not designed to be published as Python level components at all - they're either deployed directly from source control to a PaaS like Heroku or OpenShift, or else they're bundled up with all of their system level dependencies into a full Linux container image. The specification of system level dependencies hence also lives outside this file - they're part of the configuration of the deployment environment.

Given the decisions already made in PEP 518, I think the only serious contenders for a PyPA blessed "service deployment requirements" syntax are a Gemfile-inspired JSON-emitting Pythonesque DSL (potentially with a restricted builtins set), or (as suggested by @takluyver in Kenneth's gist linked above), a TOML based format that models the problem as a collection of resources with cross references between them.

Some core design principles that would apply in such a format:

- model entities as top level collections: at least `packages`, `sources`, `groups`, and probably also `environments` (to encapsulate sets of environment markers). Non-runtime dependencies would likely become new collection types (e.g. `dev_packages`)
- dependency declarations would be either strings (containing a non-empty version specifier) or else TOML tables (with the version specifier moving to an optional "version" key)
- function calls in the Pythonesque DSL would be modelled as TOML tables
- with statements in the Pythonesque DSL would modelled as nested tables
- singular cross references from packages to top level collections would be handled as strings
- plural cross references from packages to top level collections would be handled as lists
- by default, implicit cross-references would be permitted for resources with no mandatory attributes (i.e. referencing an entity creates it), but a "strict" mode would be available that threw an error

An example of that based on Donald's example above:

    [sources]
    pypi = {url:"https://pypi.org", verify_ssl=True}
    internal = "https://internal/"

    [packages]
    Django = ">= 1.10.1"
    dateutils = {}
    mything = {group="development", source="internal"}

    [packages.myotherthing]
    group="development"
    source="internal"

And showing some nesting examples:

    [groups.development.packages]
    mything = {source="internal/"}

    [sources.internal.packages]
    mything = {group="development")

    [groups.development.sources.internal.packages]
    mything = {}

As I noted on #3, I see pros and cons on both sides here, with the main advantage of TOML being that it is *much* more friendly to IDE-assisted development, and automation in general, while the procedural top-to-bottom-execution format is genuinely easier to read and write for a lot of people.

That said, it's also the case that if folks really wanted the full flexibility of Python in defining their dependencies, it would be relatively straightforward to offer a *three* stage pipeline: `Pipfile.in` (normal Python) -> `Pipfile` (TOML) -> `Pipfile.lock` (JSON). Folks could then choose between maintaining their `Pipfile` by hand, or auto-generating it from `Pipfile.in`.

To do that, the TOML format would either need a way for entries to indicate that they should be preserved when the file is regenerated from `Pipfile.in`, or else a way for it to indicate that it is auto-generated from `Pipfile.in` and hence shouldn't be edited by other means (including IDEs)

I say since `Pipfile` is Python code, then it should just end in `.py`. And similarly, use `.json` on the lock file. I don't see a reason to avoid the generic extensions. You could even use `.lock.json`.

`.lock.json` has no real precedence, but using the generic extension does: `composer.json`, `package.json`.

```
├── pip.json
├── pip.lock.json
└──setup.py
```
How about `Pipfile.lock` to `Pipfile`, and then `Pipfile` to `Pipfile.in` or `Pipfile.in.py`? This would mirror "make" syntax and the existing `MANIFEST.in`.

I'm afraid I still don't understand the difference between the canonical example and what you're looking for. The [standard pattern](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues/601#issuecomment-247143250) does reset offsets to `LATEST` (`-1`, not `-2`), but this is used only for the purpose of discovering the actual latest offset so that `1` can be subtracted from it to get the offset of the current last message instead of the "next" message to be added to the partition. In fact, this logic is nearly identical to what you posted in the OP. With this logic, you should be able to implement `consume_latest` by resetting the offset on an existing consumer to `LATEST`, then resetting that consumer's offsets to `partition.next_offset - 1` and consuming. The key insight is that the initial reset to `LATEST` can happen either on instantiation or on an existing consumer.

@tahorst thanks very much for this PR. I looked at it and confirmed that for the provided example it really speeds things up.

As you and @1fish2 mentioned, things might indeed not be quite as fast if the "unfinished" parent is not towards the beginning of the list. If it is toward the end of the list, you'd have similar performance as before.

With that in mind, I coded up a very related but different solution that takes advantage of the fact that the workflow refresh initially creates a Workflow object with "LazyFireWork" objects.

fireworks.core.launchpad.LaunchPad#get_wf_by_fw_id_lzyfw

The LazyFireWork objects only fetch data as needed - e.g., they only get the state when you need them to. Currently (before your PR), the code is fetching the state for every single parent FW - one at a time - in a "lazy" way. With your PR, the code fetches the state one at a time only until needs to, stopping when it hits an unfinished parent. So - better. However, with the new commit we bypass this altogether. This is because the #get_wf_by_fw_id_lzyfw method actually has a ``fw_states`` variable that is available to allow you to get the states of each Firework without doing any additional queries whatsoever. So, if we just use the ``fw_states`` variable to get the state rather than asking the LazyFirework to fetch the state, it should be as fast as your solution if the "unfinished" Firework is toward the beginning of the list, but also stay just as fast if the "unfinished" Firework is near the end.

The commit id is here:

	d9a71ac27cfd025af39f507137c407657e17dd38

and I will release a new FWS version on PyPI (1.7.6) that incorporates this change.

Although I am closing this PR without merging, I really want to thank you for getting us 90% of the way there and also for including the example that allowed me to really test all solutions. If you see any issues or improvements with this alternate solution, please let me know, but hopefully it should be just as fast or faster and serve your needs OK.

I've got some 3d + multichannel Cython done with loops. The issue with readability isn't so much keeping track of stuff - that should be fine, if informative variable names are used - as much as the 80 char line requirement constraining the key innermost statements. Deliberately violating PEP8 by either cutting indentation spacing in half or allowing slightly longer lines largely resolves these issues.

Also, and perhaps most importantly, by structuring in this way we're inherently getting things as ready as possible for parallelization. The entire function is factored into its minimal form/kernel. If/when Cython supports OpenMP through LLVM on newer variants of OSX, the big loop will fly with the addition of `with nogil:` and `prange`. I use this for several personal functions, and it's awesome. This structure is also likely superior for Numba/Theano/etc. gains. If fancier methods are used and then parallelization question arises, we'd probably end up retracing our steps.

My general approach is to use fancier approaches for more limited things, like operations per-pixel, but when doing anything involving local patches naive loops w/parallelization is the clear path forward.

We are currently testing, and when sending a file (everything else being equal), we got
* 132 Mb/s using the python-irodsclient
* 830 MB/s using the 'iput' command, without any specific option. The physical link is 1Gb/s, and it seems fairly optimal. 

One thing to note, is that the bandwidth is very steady using the python binding, while it's far more variable with iput.

We have the 'feeling' that somehow there's something blocking the bandwidth in the python code.

We are aware of optimisation options in iput (threading, bulk upload, datagram protocol..), and that the python code doesn't provide yet those optimisation. But we don't know if this really is the reason for such bad performance.

>> The protocol specification at http://tarantool.org/doc/box-protocol.html
>> mandates a fixed 5-byte MP_UINT value for the BODY + HEADER SIZE field in a
>> message header. That's how responses are encoded by the Tarantool server
>> and requests are encoded by other drivers (I checked tarantool-php).
>>
>> tarantool-python, however, encodes this field as a variable-length
>> MP_UINT, so it can take 1 to 5 bytes, depending on the request size. The
>> server handles it correctly, it does not enforce the fixed 5-byte field
>> length and accepts an arbitrary MP_UINT. tarantool-python expects a fixed
>> 5-byte field in responses (from Connection._read_response):
>>
>>         # Read packet length
>>         length = msgpack.unpackb(self._recv(5))
>>         # Read the packet
>>         return self._recv(length)
>>
>> Even though it works when talking to the server (because it always
>> replies with a fixed 5-bytes size values), it's still a violation of the
>> protocol spec. For example, if a proxy wants to reply to a PING request by
>> echoing PING packet data in response (which would be correct assuming
>> clients encode PING requests correctly), tarantool-python does not accept
>> such a response. In other words, it sends garbage out, but expects valid
>> replies back in.
>>
>> —

>> <https://github.com/tarantool/tarantool-python/issues/51>.

Here's a version of `CID/font.otf` with the CFF table subroutinized: [font.otf.zip](https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/psautohint/files/2204455/font.otf.zip).
This was achieved with these commands:
```
tx -cff +S +b -std font.otf CFF_tb
sfntedit -a CFF=CFF_tb font.otf
```

And here's the corresponding CID Font Resource: [font.ps.zip](https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/psautohint/files/2204469/font.ps.zip).
This kind of file relates to a CID-keyed OTF, the same way as a PFA relates to a name-keyed OTF.
It was created using this command:
```
tx -t1 font.otf font.ps
```

JS Lib: 1s 363.45864ms

**Query Results**
```
/* Run 1 */
HTTP Call: 0s 447.550193ms
JS Lib: 1s 794.338112ms

/* Run 2 */
HTTP Call: 0s 262.554809ms
JS Lib: 1s 69.181354ms

/* Run 3 */
HTTP Call: 0s 574.71431ms
JS Lib: 1s 418.149885ms
```

For reference, a straight post/res to the datastore that receives a 404 is around 70ms.

So this appears to me to show that (ignoring pingtime):
**HTTP query** Takes approximately 150ms for lookup and 400ms for query
**JS Lib** Takes approximately 1500ms for lookup and 1500ms for query.

With a datastore with a single entity.

Franking both aren't even remotely close to good enough, but especially the JS Lib. DynamoDB and MongoDB often provide sub 20ms query times on large datasets.

I've run this on a google cloud function as well, using the JS Lib and get approximately the same results. (Using `process.hrtime()` to get the actual datastore time, ignoring the function time).

I'm able to repro this too. I also see a strange delay in handling the ^C without the daemon enabled, but that eventually results in a segv.

I believe whats happening here in the daemon context is roughly along the lines of:

1) the thin client launches the daemon and connects to it, issuing an execution request to the captive engine/scheduler
2) the way the thin client handles control-C is by storing the PID of the pantsd-runner and then sending a signal to it via `os.kill()`.
3) the PID is not sent to the thin client until the pantsd-runner forks, to avoid the signal hitting pantsd directly (which will presumably crash and lose all daemon state)
4) until the fork happens, control-C is effectively [an un-retried no-op](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/f5f79ba4e667bfd37bd0b40547e944a687e5b948/src/python/pants/java/nailgun_client.py#L150)

so to fix, I think we'll have to:

1) instruct the thin client to target the daemon process for control-c signals in addition to the pantsd-runner
and
2a) be ok with crashing the daemon if a user hits control-C during the sync phase (based on presumed default signal handling)
or
2b) figure out how to plumb a smarter signal handler into the daemon that would be able to gracefully interrupt engine execution without crashing

A `_Cell` object is what is called (in this project) an *element proxy*, meaning it provides API functionality to what is essentially an `lxml` object representing an XML element.

A `_Cell` object is created when needed to satisfy an API call, such as row.cells[58]. If you call that twice, you'll get two different objects; however, the underlying `lxml` object will be the same in each case. No new table cells are created.

If you want to tell whether two `_Cell` objects are wrapping the same cell element, you can use:

```python
cell_1._tc == cell_2._tc
```

Which will evaluate `True` if both are wrapping the same cell element.

A `_Cell` object does not store any data other than the `tc` element reference. Any properties read or write directly to the XML object. So you should think of the `_Cell` object as a handy wrapper of functionality over the XML element rather than a separate data item.

Hi @jayoshih, @benjaoming,

We do have initial apidocs! http://learningequalityricecooker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/py-modindex.html 

It's now updated every time we push to the repo.

> Should it be py2+3? Yes

I prefer if we support only Py3 -- a lot of recent Linux distributions now bundle python 3 in (Ubuntu, Arch Linux, Fedora etc.), and we don't have a strong need to support older versions. Not to mention that we can take advantage of newer python libraries such as `async def`

I don't think we also need to be pure python. Especially since we might do a lot of preprocessing before uploading to the cc server (transcoding videos, compressing files etc.), I think runtime speed and features trump compatibility with limited installations.

I think the use case for `ricecooker` is to be able to install it like this:

`pip install ricecooker`

I agree with @jayoshih that we have no need to bundle packages, since this is meant to be used with Internet! (at least for now)

Thus, we do need `setup.py`, and upload this to PyPI eventually.

We can perhaps call it `urn` or `uri`.

`showMementoAtDatetime()` handles requests for `/20171201094013/http://example.com`.

It's really a wrapper to the generic `show_uri()`.

`showMemento()` does the "closest datetime" resolution, i.e., requests for `/memento/*/http://example.com`.

There is definitely some duplicate code and redundant functionality, which stems from progressively adding the functionality.

The sliced TimeMap functionality also needs to serve the web UI when a 404 for a URI-M is encountered. I think the `/timemap/` endpoint ought to be isolated from this.

We also have `getLinkHeaderAbbreviatedTimeMap()` for the Link response headers. Similar logic can be used for the sliced TMs.

I think it should be the other way where replay is given precedence as the default command. Or better yet, `ipwb` should show the help text. `ipwb index` and `ipwb replay` should work as expected, while keeping space for other sub-commands in future.

I did testing using an NI-5441 and spoke to an NI-FGEN developer that worked on the latest PXIe-5433 family, which uses a new driver stack.

* The older DAQmx-based like 5441 devices do indeed use Big-I routing subsystem which allows inter device routing for clocking signals.
* The newer devices like 5433 do not use Big-I routing subsystem for clocking signals; only for triggers and events. This makes sense as the PXI Trigger bus is not suitable for fast, low jitter clocking signals that our devices require.

THANK YOU! This has bugged me for a while.  I love the efficiency and the solution (and having tests); I don't like, however, adding a "context" attribute to Note -- I'm trying to make Note simpler and I believe that this will make it more complex for only a single use case as it's currently written.

What I suggest is that we make it a little less efficient, but more powerful and conceptually simpler -- have the Note in the chord be created as before, let's make in pitch.py (but not in pitch.Pitch object) a simplifyMultipleEnharmonics routine that can be quite powerful.  It takes in a list of pitch objects (not Notes) or numbers (pitch class or MIDI) and it can have different selection criteria, of which what could be the default for now (and only one that would need to be implemented for this pull request unless you wanted to do more) could be 'optimizeConsonance'.  It should take an optional Key or KeySignature object as well.  It should return a list of pitches so that there's a one-to-one correspondence between the original pitch index and the new one.  Probably all pitches should be new objects even if no change needs to be made to the pitch.

Chord could have its own method "simplifyEnharmonics" (default inPlace=False, but called with True on __init__) that takes care to replace the pitches on the Notes. Then this routine could be called from Chord if all(pitches) are numbers to change the pitches of the notes.  Mixtures of numbers and pitch names/Note objects would not get optimized (who would do that?).  It would allow for all notes to be considered before making a determination rather than determining the best spelling at any given moment.

The reason I want this approach, besides leaving Notes out of it, is that it could be used, for instance, to spell MIDI input as best as possible (something I desperately need).  A quick parse could allow for determining the key of the piece (.analyze('key')) and this could be used to minimize written accidentals (using the KeySignature to determine which accidentals would need to be written). Another approach could be to first minimize augmented intervals, then to optimize for consonance, then to minimize unisons, then seconds, then minimize diminished intervals.  For writing out a chord, the minimization may be most important.  C# + Eb may be preferable to C#-D# even though the first is a diminished interval and the latter is a M2.  Both should be preferred to Db-D# of course.

This is something that I've wanted/needed/desired for some time but haven't gotten around to it.  I hope that it's okay that I'm asking for it to do a bit more even though the current approach definitely solves your problem. Thanks!

> large backlog in memory. So some cleverness is required.

> While we're at it, another HPC-friendly feature would be the ability to

Having a text field named `"error"` seems pretty sane. We shouldn't be completely helpless in the face of such an unusual and novel error format. :wink:

@pipermerriam this has reached the "make it run" phase of the implementation in the sense that it at least passes all tests. :tada:  Commit history is still a mess and there's still other work left to get done.

I'm reaching out to get your opinion about a couple of things.

**1. Regarding the discussed context manager API**

I'm not yet 100 % sure we want it but since I'm still very new to this, there's a high chance that I'm wrong, hence I'm reaching out to discuss it.

Ok, so why would we want a context manager API in the first place? Because we assumed otherwise it would be easy to use the API wrong (e.g. forget to start recording changes, forget to write them to the underlying db etc).

This doesn't seem to be the case though. The way this works with the current implementation is  that the `JournalDB` is always recording changes by default. If we call `persist()` the changes will be written back to the underlying db and a new recording starts automatically.

But there's another reason. I noticed that there exists an integration test that uses a `ChainDB` that does not use `JournalDB` at all. In fact this thing is called [`NonJournaledAsyncChainDB`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/7122c02ef6dde72c9921a3c6d06d2b0cc8635196/evm/db/chain.py#L680-L697) and basically for the test that uses this every call inside `ChainDB` that boils down to `self.db.<something>` will hit the `MemoryDB`. I'm not sure if that is a thing that is only relevant for the tests and could easily be changed on the testing side or if it is actually a hard requirement that things are supposed to work in such a scenario.

However, what that basically means is that if the `JournalDB` gets a context manager API and uses it within `ChainDB` then `MemoryDB` would need that same API even though it makes probably less sense for `MemoryDB` to have this kind of API.

In order to work around that, what I did for now, is that I expose the raw passed `base_db` as a property on the `ChainDB` next to the usual `db` property (which is supposed to be wrapped as `JournalDB` but sometimes ends up not being one).

This allows that certain actions within the `ChainDB` can simply call `self.base_db.set(...)` and `self.base_db.delete(...)` to bypass the journaling/caching altogether. This is based on the assumption that certain actions simply don't need the journaling anyway.

If that would be a viable way forward then I'd propose we go further and rename the properties to `non_journaled_db` and `journaled_db` or something similar to make it more clear that there are two different ways to access the database.

Btw, I just moved enough calls from `self.db.<method>` to `self.base_db.<method>` to get the test suite passing but I guess there may be even more that could be moved to always hit the non-journaled db.

And my third and last aspect of this would be that there really just is a single call to `persist()` in [`apply_transaction`](https://github.com/cburgdorf/py-evm/blob/d346bd2b059d91c11b436cebbae1a556db70eec1/evm/vm/base.py#L89) anway. This is again, based on the assumption that the transaction processing is the only part of the system that actually needs the functionality of undoing changes.

**Regarding usage of [`ChainMap`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html#collections.ChainMap)

I haven't looked much into that yet because I first want to get the basics right but from the docs it says

>A ChainMap groups multiple dicts or other mappings together to create a single, updateable view. If no maps are specified, a single empty dictionary is provided so that a new chain always has at least one mapping.

>The underlying mappings are stored in a list. That list is public and can be accessed or updated using the maps attribute. There is no other state.

If the underlying mechanics work as a `List[Dict[TKey, TVal]` isn't that a deal breaker because it would mean we could only reference to the changesets (the inner dict) by there index in the list whereas we really need the functionality of referencing them through a `UUID` hence we currently use a `Dict[UUID, Dict[TKey, TVal]]`?

**Regarding terminology**

I changed the terminology to reflect the new model of the `JournalDB` as I mentioned before. I personally think the old terminology just doesn't apply anymore because we previously had it as follows

`.snapshot()` - snapshot the **original values** and return a checkpoint id that allows **going back** to the original values (because changes were immediately applied to the underlying db).

`revert(snapshot)` - which really meant *revert to `<snapshot-id>`* as in: get all the original values from the `snapshot` and reset the underlying database to it.

That's just not how the new model works so I changed the terminology to 

`.record()` - starts recording and returns a `changeset_id` where all **changes** are recorded in. Nothing is written to the db.

`.discard(changeset_id)` - This simply throws away all **changes** that were recorded in the given `changeset`. Again, the underlyng db wasn't hit at all.

Alright, that's it from me so far. Sorry for the long blogging :grimacing:

Well first of all in the Windows and CMD.EXE world the `PATH` is `;` delimited not `:` delimited.

So in your `etc\conda\activate.d\env_var.bat`:

```
set PATH=C:\Local\Programs\R-3.3.2\bin;%PATH%
```

And in your `etc\conda\deactivate.d\env_var.bat`:

```
set PATH=%PATH:C:\Local\Programs\R-3.3.2\bin;=%
```

I understand what happened.  And yes, it was conda's fault...

Between conda 4.4.0 and conda 4.4.3, a `conda update conda` operation would install conda in the current active environment.  Basically `conda update` was exactly the same as `conda install`, which is incorrect.  If a package isn't already installed in the target environment, `conda update` should complain and refuse to proceed.

That was fixed in 4.4.4.  It looks like you managed to get conda installed in your `conda_test` environment though, likely because of this bug.

Try

    source activate conda_test
    conda remove conda

Make sure the conda package is being removed from your environment located at `/home/bhayden/software/anaconda/envs/conda_test`.  And then poke around from there...

If you don't get anywhere after that... Share with me the output of `conda list -n conda_test`

## Solved
`conda install ipyparallel` broke my conda installation
To fix I needed to move my entire conda directory and reinstall i.e.
```bash
mv ~/.anaconda ~/.anacondatmp
# download conda from https://www.continuum.io/downloads
```

## Top tip for next time
Immediately after you install, create a virtual environment so that you can easily roll back any changes you make. e.g. from a fresh install:
```bash
conda create --name py27 --clone root
```

Long story,
`obspy` had some problems building conda binaries for the most recent version on 32bit Linux, and the older version (fallback) packages were obviously removed from `main` channel for some reason by one of us (probably because on binstar the selection drop-downs etc. are pretty useless if the project's files are spread over several pages).

Actually, I'm one of the obspy developers, it's just such a jungle to keep track of all packaging/distribution options, and how often do you run into somebody trying to install on 32bit Linux.. -_-

First off, this is a conda-build issue, not a conda issue. 

Second, conda-build doesn't attempt to create directories for you. The PREFIX starts empty. You need to `mkdir -p` any directories that you use in your build.sh. It is a common mistake, but at least if you don't forget your slashes like you did here, you should get a pretty obvious warning about it.

This is by design.  Up through conda 4.3, conda will hold onto the python major-minor version that's in the root environment when creating new environments that contain python packages.  If you want a version of python that's different from your root environment, you'll need to specify it.  So in your case

    conda create -n unicycler unicycler python=3

should work.

@kraigher I agree that it is easy to make the conversion to a VUnit-compatible testbench. My concern would but that by doing so, that running the testbench (with or without the VUnit runner) now requires the VUnit library. 

When using the VUnit runner approach, this is not a problem because VUnit library is automatically included in the compilation of dependencies. However, if running a testbench via simulator directly (without VUnit runner), the users must first compile the VUnit library sources into an appropriately named library and ensure it is mapped into their project.  This isn't insurmountable via training, but it not a "transparent"/backwards compatible change to add VUnit support to existing testbenches.  For example, if I were to update existing testbench code of widely shared modules so that they could be tested via VUnit runner, now anyone else that uses those shared modules must also learn where to get VUnit sources, compile, and map. If they didn't realize they needed to do this, all of the testbenches would now appear broken.

In my experience, managing 3rd party libraries for VHDL is a painful setup step -- it is just a lot easier whenever user has the libraries by default (e.g. those bundled with simulator, _ieee, osvvm_ and some vendor specific libraries).

That said, I don't know if there is a good solution for VUnit to avoid this - perhaps it is just the price of adoption.

Hi @madhukargilla 

- [Get by id is available](http://azure-sdk-for-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ref/azure.mgmt.resource.resources.v2016_09_01.operations.html#azure.mgmt.resource.resources.v2016_09_01.operations.ResourcesOperations.get_by_id) in the `azure-mgmt-resource` package, otherwise it will provide you a `dict` of the properties, not a Python object like a regular "get" will.

Yeah I think adding that is pretty reasonable.   We're looking to move away from yaml soon to fix some stuff, let's go with `-c` `--config` as a cli parameter.  I think you'll want to augment each of these commands (`run`, `install`, `autoupdate`).

Here's how I tend to do local development against hooks (when necessary, usually I only do it for the first iteration of the hook and then rely on other tests in the repository itself to manage the hooks).

Initialize my git repository, Let's say it's at `/home/asottile/workspace/hook-repo`

Do some development (on `master` branch -- yeah this part is not the greatest) and commit a `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` file.

Run `git rev-parse HEAD` to find your current revision (we'll only need to do this for the first time, we'll rely on autoupdate from then on).

With this revision, temporarily add to a consuming repository the following:

```yaml
-   repo: /home/asottile/workspace/hook-repo
    sha: <<the result of git rev-parse HEAD>>
    hooks:
    -   your-hook-id
```

Continue to iterate by doing the following:

- In your `hook-repo` make whatever changes are necessary
- Run `git commit ...` (since pre-commit works by resetting to a specific revision, an actual commit / tag is necessary for `sha: ...`)
- In your consuming repo, run `pre-commit autoupdate --bleeding-edge` to update to the latest revision.  This will automatically set the `sha: ...` to the latest revision on the `master` branch (without `--bleeding-edge`, it will seek out the nearest tag).
- Then you can test with `pre-commit run --all-files` or `pre-commit run --files ...` etc.
- If you want to do a proper pull request to your `hook-repo`: once satisfied, in `hook-repo` rename the `master` branch to something appropriate for a pull request and rebase / etc. your branch.
    - You can rename a branch by running `git branch -m my-new-branch-name`

You shouldn't need to push if you're referring to the local filesystem (just commit) as a filesystem location is a valid clone source.

Let me know if any part of this is unclear or what can be added to the documentation (or if you want to PR the docs, they [live here](https://github.com//pre-commit/pre-commit.github.io) :)

I am struggling with this issue as well for some time now. The kernel keeps running your job on the server, but there is no way to see the console output after closing the browser.

My workaround was to write all my logs to a file, so that when my browser closes (indeed when a lot of logs come through browser it hangs up too) I can see the kernel job process by opening the log file (the log file can be open using Jupyter too).

```
    #!/usr/bin/python
    import time
    import datetime
    import logging
    
    logger = logging.getLogger()
    
    def setup_file_logger(log_file):
        hdlr = logging.FileHandler(log_file)
        formatter = logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s')
        hdlr.setFormatter(formatter)
        logger.addHandler(hdlr) 
        logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
    
    def log(message):
        #outputs to Jupyter console
        print('{} {}'.format(datetime.datetime.now(), message))
        #outputs to file
        logger.info(message)
    
    setup_file_logger('out.log')
    
    for i in range(10000):
        log('Doing hard work here i=' + str(i))
        log('Taking a nap now...')
        time.sleep(1000)
```

Did some more testing with this. Performance drops quite a bit from 2K msg/sec to 300 msg/sec.
This is due to heavy acquaint_schema call inside schemaless_writer on every message as well as:

[https://github.com/lewis-grimmett-zefr/confluent-kafka-python/blob/fastavro-optimizations/confluent_kafka/avro/__init__.py#L26-L28](url)

Hashing avro.schema.RecordSchema using its str is pretty heavy for large schemas (our schema is 10KB), this slows down cached schema lookups.

Bypassing both of these code paths brought performance back up to around 2K msg/sec.

the solution is:
```
from marshmallow import Schema, validates_schema
from marshmallow.fields import String

class ASchema(Schema):
    q = String(required=True)

class BSchema(Schema):
    name = String(required=True)

class XSchema(Schema):
    type = String(required=True)
    query = Dict(required=True)
    
    @validates_schema
    def validate_query(self, data):
        globals()['%sSchema' % data['type'].capitalize()](strict=True).load(data['query'])


x_schema = XSchema(partial=False)

x_schema.load(dict(type='a', query={'q': 'python'}))
# UnmarshalResult(data={'query': {'q': 'python'}, 'type': u'a'}, errors={})
x_schema.load(dict(type='b', query={'name': 'schema'}))
# UnmarshalResult(data={'query': {'name': 'schema'}, 'type': u'b'}, errors={})
```

@emojiface-ops I had a chance to look at the perf test project this evening and have a couple of observations:

1. The code was using `ModelViewSet` from `rest_framework`. If you use the `ModelViewSet` from `rest_framework_json_api.views`, then you don't have to do the prefetch directly.
2. From what I saw in the debug toolbar, the code you provided *did* do the prefetch. The result of the prefetch is the third query on the `emoji_collection` table that has a WHERE clause full of IDs. Proof of the prefetch is visible if the line is commented out. Uncommented: 23 queries. Commented out: 42 queries.
3. Adding more to the `prefetch_related` can drop the query count from 23 to 4. I changed the code to the following:

```python
        self.queryset = self.queryset.prefetch_related(
            'emoji_collection', 'emoji_collection__emojis')
```

@aidanlister noted the verbosity of this restriction (i.e. needing to add more included stuff to the prefetch even for the nested stuff) in previously at https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/issues/337#issuecomment-297335342.

As I consider the options for dealing with prefetch, I think I'm leaning toward @aidanlister's suggestion of being explicit. Having code that magically determines what to prefetch does sound nice in theory, however, I agree that Django favors explicit settings, and I think that code to automatically build the prefetch set might have some weird consequences.

One thing I don't understand is the gains from having an extra `prefetch_for_includes`. Are there some benefits from those class settings that I'm not seeing vs. using `prefetch_related` directly on the queryset?

@bjodah, Lambdify's `__call__` has considerable overhead.

Here's some python code that I wrote,
```Python

outputs_ravel = list(itertools.chain(*[output.tolist() for output in outputs]))
cb = symengine.Lambdify(inputs, outputs_ravel, backend="llvm")

def func(*args):
    result = []
    n = numpy.empty(len(outputs_ravel))
    t = cb.unsafe_real(np.concatenate([arg.ravel() for arg in args]), n)
    start = 0
    for output in outputs:
        elems = reduce(mul, output.shape)
        result.append(n[start:start+elems].reshape(output.shape))
        start += elems
    return result
    
return lambda *args: func(*args)
```
This makes SymEngine as fast as PyDy's cythonize. Updated the timings here, https://github.com/symengine/symengine/pull/1094#issuecomment-254212768

> I have worked it out!

Good! What was the problem, in case anybody else googles for this error? I assume you forgot the `trainable=True` flag in your `params = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(network, trainable=True)` call, when collecting the parameters for the update dictionary. This is needed so `get_all_params` only returns the parameters that participate in the forward pass. Without filtering for the `trainable` tag, it will also return parameters that are only important at prediction time (in this case, the exponential running averages over means and variances). This is independent of the dimensionality of the data you batch-normalize.

> My question is how to write efficient code for training on large scale datasets?

The MNIST example already sports an `iterate_minibatches()` function, a generator function that returns one mini-batch at a time. You can use the same scheme for iterating over files that are *not* yet loaded into memory. For example, you could dump your data into multiple files that are then accessed in random order, or into a single large `.npy` file that is loaded with `np.load(..., mmap_mode='r')` to be read as a memory-mapped file (this would allow you to use the same iteration code as before, since it can be treated like a regular numpy array).

> Specifically, what is the best way to prepare mini-batches (including real time data augmentation) in order to keep the GPU busy?

To do so, you can run your generator function in a background thread: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/12#issuecomment-59494251
Theano will release the GIL when waiting for GPU operations to finish, so this allows your generator function to be run in parallel.
Your generator function can include data augmentation as well. Often you will find it more convenient to stack multiple generator functions -- one for reading the data, one for performing agumentation X, one for performing augmentation Y, ... I've got example code of that structure for real-time data augmentation of audio spectrograms here: http://ofai.at/~jan.schlueter/code/augment/

Important caveat: If your data is stored in HDF5 format, any file I/O will take the GIL again, which will reduce the amount of parallelism a lot. You will need to resort to the `multiprocessing` module then (requires minimal changes to my background thread example). This, in turn, will incur some overhead for transferring the data from one process to another via pickling and unpickling it. `joblib` includes a workaround for numpy arrays that resorts to memory-mapped files for faster transfer.

Hope this helps!

If you work your way through the code of Jet, you can make some overrides yourself. However, it would be good to see support included.

I propose this code as a replacement for the jet/forms.py lookup function:
`
def lookup(self):
	import operator
	from jet.utils import get_model_instance_label
	from django.db.models import Q

	if self.cleaned_data['q']:

		if getattr(self.model_cls, 'autocomplete_search_fields') and getattr(self.model_cls, 'autocomplete_search_query'):
			raise Exception("The model cannot have both an autocomplete_search_fields and autocomplete_search_query function.")

		elif getattr(self.model_cls, 'autocomplete_search_query'):
			qs = self.model_cls.autocomplete_search_query(self.cleaned_data['q'])

		elif getattr(self.model_cls, 'autocomplete_search_fields'):
			qs = self.model_cls.objects
			search_fields = self.model_cls.autocomplete_search_fields()
			filter_data = [Q((field + '__icontains', self.cleaned_data['q'])) for field in search_fields]
			qs = qs.filter(reduce(operator.or_, filter_data))

	else:
		qs = self.model_cls.objects.none()

	limit = self.cleaned_data['page_size'] or 100
	page = self.cleaned_data['page'] or 1
	offset = (page - 1) * limit

	items = list(map(lambda instance: {
		'id': instance.pk,
		'text': get_model_instance_label(instance)
	}, qs.all()[offset:offset + limit]))
	total = qs.count()

	return items, total

`
This way you could define a autocomplete_search_query function in your models.

I've done some more digging on this. I think the issue is that in `datastores.riotapi.common`, only the method rate limiters are implemented per-region, and the `"application"` rate limiter is global across all routes(:+1:) and regions(:-1:). 

Unfortunately the app and method limiters are integrated very differently and I'm not sure it will be a simple change to have app limiters per-region like the method limiters. One possible quick solution could be to change `RiotApiService._get_rate_limiter` to return both the method and app limiters, and then adjust the signature of `RiotApiService._get` to handle the parameter change. I think this would be an OK solution as `_get_rate_limiter` already receives the region as the `platform` parameter, and already does the job of initialising method limiters if they're not set already. 

I might have a stab at fixing this, but in any sort of interim the easiest fix is to run each region in a separate process.

Re getting this working, I think some snippets from mike42/escpos-php#35 will help here. A TM-U220B single-density bit image print-out (using `ESC *`), as an example:

![2016-03-tux](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2080552/13774422/15f330c4-eaf3-11e5-816b-33e632af825e.jpg)

`ESC *` prints out the image in a series of rows, and needs some line feed trickery to work the same between printers ('feed n pixels' after each row). The command works on the older Epson U220B, newer TM-T20 and low-end Zijian ZJ5890T (which support double-density graphics, unlike the TM U220).

I tried some things on my end, but it's been pretty well behaved in various configurations.

> However, copy/move constructors are not tracked at all

They are actually tracked (and pretty much have to be to avoid having too many destructions): the `constructor_stats.move_created()`/`.copy_created()` calls do that.  The tests no longer check the specific number of move/copy constructors, because that's exactly the thing that varies across compilers.  However, each constructor--including the copy/move constructors--has to be matched with a destructor for the test to succeed.  Copy ellision is going to reduce the number of copy/move constructors, but is also going to reduce the number of destructors, so the total remains balance.

> It's important for the performance of expensive-to-copy objects, so I can see it being useful to have at least some tracking

For tests where it is important, checking `cstats.copy_constructions` or `cstats.move_constructions` is possible--e.g. perhaps a test against cstats.copy_constructions == 0.  (I forgot to expose this to python, 0c5337f fixes that).

> We're kind of lucky that the CPython implementation is reasonably predictable and it calls destructors as expected in the tests.

We really aren't lucky in that respect—the current master test code doesn't test the order/timing of destructions at all, merely that they happen eventually (the test output gets sorted before being compared).  This PR improves on that aspect of the tests quite a bit by invoking gc.collect() inside `cstats.alive()` to try to reduce the amount of luck required and make the whole thing more predictable.  (And actually, now that I think about it, the sorting of output may no longer be needed as well; I shall investigate).

data_files=[

And then `pybind11.get_include()` would need to return this directory.

* Not completely related, but at some point it would be nice to bundle the CMake files with the PyPI package, similarly to what has been done for the `conda-forge` package. I'm not sure if these could be included as anything other than `data_files`.

The pre- and post- hooks are only run when a cert is actually being renewed.  Since your cert isn't due for renewal, nothing is happening and no hooks are run.

To test things out, you could temporarily edit your crontab to add `--force-renewal`.  Just remember to remove the flag again once you've confirmed it's working as intended!

Closing for now, please reopen if the cert is being renewed and the hook isn't running.

> Changing the way that we create the requirements doesn't change the way Tox runs the tests. Shouldn't I build some condition in tox.ini that if $CERTBOT_OLDEST is set that we don't run the azure-dns tests?

Changing the requirements should change the packages used when tox runs tests on this plugin. By changing the requirements to versions that satisfy all dependencies, tests should pass when tox runs tests.

> If we want to have certbot-dns-azure to remain unpackaged, What does it mean exactly? I am not sure I know where the DNS are packaged other than when installing them in the dev environment...

This doesn't mean much technically for your PR. It's just the reason breaking our normal practices and requiring a newer version of `requests` in `local-oldest-requirements.txt` is OK.

What it means outside of this repo though is many Linux distros won't be able to package this plugin because of how strict its dependency requirements are. We can still put the plugin up on PyPI, Docker Hub, and the more bleeding edge distros will be able to package it.

We solved it by creating a config file (etc/apache2/conf-available/certbot.conf), where we added the following content:

> AliasMatch ^/.well-known/acme-challenge/(.*)$ /var/www/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/$1
> Alias /.well-known/acme-challenge/ /var/www/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/
> <Directory "/var/www/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/">
>     Options None
>     AllowOverride None
>     ForceType text/plain
>     RedirectMatch 404 "^(?!/\.well-known/acme-challenge/[\w-]{43}$)"
> </Directory>

Somewhat related to this: 

I started on a new project using Starlette, and I'm currently trying to figure out a good way of handling the routing/templating/views. Below is my first attempt to come up with a CBV pattern using the functionality `asgi_application` provides. 


```python
import asyncio
from starlette import Request, Response
from starlette.routing import Router, Path
from starlette.types import Receive, Send, Scope


class View:
    def __init__(self, scope: Scope):
        self.scope = scope

    async def __call__(self, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        request = Request(self.scope, receive)
        request_method = request.method if request.method != "HEAD" else "GET"
        func = getattr(self, request_method.lower(), None)
        if func is None:
            raise Exception(
                f"Method {request_method} is not implemented for this view handler."
            )
        if asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(func):
            response = await func(request)
        else:
            response = func(request)
        await response(receive, send)


class HomepageView(View):
    # async def get(self, request):
    #     response = Response("Hello async world!", media_type="text/plain")
    #     return response

    def get(self, request):
        response = Response("Hello sync world!", media_type="text/plain")
        return response

app = Router([Path("/", app=HomepageView, methods=["GET"])])
```

It would need to be finessed a bit and the `asgi_application` logic could be pulled out of the view class itself and implemented using a `make_asgi` method in the way you described above - think something along the lines of Django's `as_view()`.

Would something like this be in the scope of Starlette?

Notes:
- `locked amount` is updated *together* with the `merkle tree`, we could update the locked amount without changing the tree, but then the tree would have unbounded size.
- The lock removal is controlled *only by the sender of the unidirectional channel*, the message from B to A is not required nor trusted, it's just a hint to A informing that a lock may be removed, and B won't stall the channel waiting for a block confirmation.

Different designs:
- It's viable to use only the `RemoveLock` message, without the message from B informing that a lock may be removed. Stalls can be avoided by A by delaying the message a block or two.
- The hint from B to A may be the block number instead of the lockhash.

Python's stdlib isn't a good example. IDNA is a messy, here are some *fun* examples and guide lines

* there are multiple IDNA encoding variants that are partly incompatible (e.g. ``ß`` is encoded differently)
* IDNA variant depends on TLD, e.g. ``.de`` is always IDNA 2008, ``.com`` may still be IDNA 2003 (unverified)
* user facing applications should use UTS46 instead of IDNA 2008. UTS46 normalizes upper case chars to lower case chars.
* TLDs often have additional restrictions to avoid homoglyphic confusion attacks
* IDNA encoding/decoding is not a bijective mapping, Python stdlib treats encoding/decoding as bijective
* IDNA for partial wildcard matches is not defined
* partial wildcards (``www*.example.org``) is deprecated and no longer supported by some browsers
* DNS and X.509 always use IDN A-labels (punycoded ASCII representation), user facing code is usually IDN U-label (unicode)

For IDNA 2008 / UTS46 you have to split the FQDN on ``.``, encode each label, ignore trailing ``*`` and join them together again.

It's not a checker job. The problem is that there's no way to specify same type for input/output and attribute. I believe attributeproto does the same thing as tensorproto. We're adding ints/strings/floats into AttributeProto as shortcuts, actually they can be presented by tensor too. 

The only thing special in AttributeProto now is "Graph" type, which should be cut and using "String" to refer its graph name.

It's not that big change, as we're now only having If/Loop experimental operators using this "graph" attribute. We should do it as early as possible before we're using them more in stable scenarios/ops.
